events.text: Not a catastrophic collapse, but incremental. Like a person slowly losing stamina over a long time. The appearance was still there, but each hour it was less robust than the previous one.

The weakening rate was engineered not to trigger immediate alarms. The Ark's monitoring system would classify it as normal power fluctuation. By the time it weakened below critical threshold and the alarm sounded, it would be too late.

Elias stood on a high point in the Wasteland, watching the Ark's silhouette in the distance. He could see the shield's light veil. Over the past few hours, its color had slowly shifted from stable blue-white to something darker, thinner, like a repeatedly stretched membrane approaching its breaking point.
The Ark's inhabitants didn't know. They thought it was just another power fluctuation, thought it would auto-repair, thought it would hold like every other time.

They didn't know there was no auto-repair this time. This weakening had a written endpoint, and that endpoint connected to a target marker already inscribed in the AI Legion's combat directive system. That marker was there now, waiting for the shield to weaken to a penetrable strength. The calculation was precise—as precise as all of Soren's calculations.
Elias stood on that high ground for a long time, watching the veil grow thinner and thinner, watching a countdown he didn't know how to stop from every direction.

She was still there, near the corridor exit, beside that cold body. When he left, she was still kneeling. He stood for one second, then followed Soren. He couldn't say which was right—that second of standing or that second of leaving. But in that second, he chose to follow because he thought he could stop him.

He stood here now. Soren's plan was halfway through. The shield was weakening. The Ark's people didn't know. Lyra didn't know. Everyone alive in the Wasteland didn't know. Only he knew. Only he stood here, watching that veil grow more fragile by the moment, and knowing there was only one person who could do what needed to be done.
She had nothing left. Nothing. But he needed her now. He didn't know if she could still stand. But he knew there was no other way.
He descended from the high ground, adjusted his direction, and walked back.
In the distance, the Ark's shield continued emitting its increasingly faint glow against the dark sky, like a candle nearing the end of its burn, quiet and without struggle, moving toward extinction.
And in the city, people still didn't know darkness was approaching.
